name: Biochemical Characterization of an Alcohol Dehydrogenase From Ralstonia sp.
description: Kulig et al. (2013), Biotechnology and Bioengineering 110(7):1838–1848. DOI: 10.1002/bit.24857. Biochemical characterization of the NADPH-dependent alcohol dehydrogenase from Ralstonia sp. DSM 6428 (RADH), including purification, stabilization by CaCl2, pH and temperature optima, substrate range (aldehydes, ketones, alcohols), and steady-state kinetics for selected substrates including 2-hydroxypropiophenone (2-HPP) enantiomers.

identity.name: Alcohol dehydrogenase from Ralstonia sp. (RADH)
catalyzed_reaction: Stereoselective reduction of ketones and aldehydes to alcohols (NADPH-dependent)
Reduction of alpha-hydroxy ketones with high enantio- and diastereoselectivity
identity.origin_organism: Ralstonia sp. DSM 6428 (also known as Cupriavidus sp.)

purification_method: Size exclusion chromatography (second step) to remove NaCl and adjust CaCl2 concentration

activity.determination_method: 1 U = amount of enzyme catalyzing 1 μmol NADPH production or consumption per min at 30°C

workflow_details.cell_disruption_process: Cell disruption as described previously (Kulig et al., 2012)
workflow_details.separation_method: Anion exchange chromatography followed by size exclusion chromatography
workflow_details.source_of_cellfree_extract: E. coli BL21(DE3) expressing RADH from pET-22b(+)

additives: 50% (v/v) glycerol (best option, retains 120% activity after 80 days)
storage.notes: Frozen in TEA-HCl buffer 10 mmol/L pH 7.5 at -20°C guarantees full stability ≥80 days. Glycerol 50% v/v leads to increased activity (+40%) during first 4 days. Lyophilized enzyme requires hours of reactivation in buffer before use.

biocatalyst.notes: RADH is the first case where Ca2+ ions were found to drastically enhance stability of an NADPH-dependent ADH. Native molecular weight by SEC: ~75 kDa suggesting trimeric structure (single subunit: 26.7 kDa). TRIS-HCl buffer inhibits activity compared to TEA-HCl and glycine-NaOH. ZnCl2 reduces activity to 39%; CuCl2 and MnCl2 to 75%; CoCl2 and NiCl2 to 85-86%. Half-life without Ca2+: ~6 h at RT; with 0.8 mmol/L CaCl2 during purification: 163 h at 8°C.

reaction_components.notes: Standard substrate for reduction assay; specific activity 4.89 U/mg
Standard substrate for oxidation assay; specific activity at 10 mmol/L: 8.36 U/mg; at 100 mmol/L: 11.46 U/mg
Preferred substrate; specific activity 362.6 U/mg at 10 mmol/L; Km=1.6 mmol/L; Vmax=486.8 U/mg; substrate inhibition Ki=16.4 mmol/L; de >99% (syn product)
Less preferred enantiomer; specific activity 17.1 U/mg at 10 mmol/L; Km=3.6 mmol/L; Vmax=23.1 U/mg; de >99% (anti product)
Highest reductase activity among tested aldehydes: 23.31 U/mg
Highest specific activity among ketones: 12.62 U/mg
Highest specific activity among alcohols (oxidation): 18.83 U/mg
Cofactor for reduction. Km (benzaldehyde reduction): 0.005 mmol/L (n=1.6, sigmoidal); Km (cyclohexanone reduction): 0.008 mmol/L (n=2.8, sigmoidal). Optimal range 0.05–0.2 mmol/L.
Cofactor for oxidation. Km (cyclohexanol oxidation): 0.06 mmol/L; Ki (surplus inhibition): 11.4 mmol/L. Optimal concentration 0.8 mmol/L.

Ca2+ ions significantly stabilize RADH. First reported case of Ca2+-dependent stabilization of an NADPH-dependent ADH. 0.2 mmol/L used during chromatography; 0.8 mmol/L in all activity and stability assays.
Standard buffer for reduction assay at pH 7.5; preferred over TRIS-HCl which reduces activity
Standard buffer for oxidation assay at pH 10.5

operation_mode.notes: Initial rate activity measurements in 1 mL cuvettes for 60 s at 30°C. Triplicate measurements throughout.

monoliquid.solvent_description: Aqueous buffer (TEA-HCl pH 7.5 for reduction; glycine-NaOH pH 10.5 for oxidation; TRIS-HCl pH 9.0 for oxidation kinetics)
monoliquid.further_additives: CaCl2 0.8 mmol/L in all assay buffers

temperature.notes: Standard assay temperature. Activity increases ~linearly from 15 to 45°C by 400%. Stability optimum 8–15°C (half-life 130–138 h with 0.8 mmol/L CaCl2). Cold inactivation at 0°C (half-life 41 h). At 25°C half-life ~80 h. For bioorganic synthesis 15–30°C recommended.

detection.detected_how: Spectrophotometric assay following NADPH at 340 nm

pH.notes: Reduction pH optimum: broad, pH 6–9.5. Oxidation pH optimum: narrow, pH 10–11.5. pH stability optimum: pH 5.5–8.0 (half-lives 60–70 h at 25°C). Enzyme unstable above pH 9. TRIS-HCl reduces activity vs TEA-HCl.

activity.notes: Highest for aldehyde reduction: 2-bromobenzaldehyde 23.31 U/mg; benzaldehyde 4.89 U/mg; 3-fluorobenzaldehyde 11.70 U/mg; acetaldehyde 0.05 U/mg; formaldehyde n.d. Highest for ketones: 3-methylcyclohexanone 12.62 U/mg; cyclohexanone 9.78 U/mg; acetone 0.01 U/mg. Highest for alcohol oxidation: 2-cyclohexenol 18.83 U/mg; cyclohexanol 11.46 U/mg (100 mmol/L). (R)-2-HPP 362.6 U/mg; (S)-2-HPP 17.1 U/mg.

kinetics.notes: Full Table V: Benzaldehyde Km(NADPH)=0.005 mmol/L, Vmax=5.6 U/mg, n=1.6. Cyclohexanone Km(NADPH)=0.008 mmol/L, Vmax=17.6 U/mg, n=2.8. NADP+ Ki(cyclohexanol)=11.4 mmol/L. Cyclohexanol oxidation Vmax=7.7 U/mg, Km=5.2 mmol/L. Benzaldehyde reduction Vmax=12.6 U/mg, Km=15.6 mmol/L. Cyclohexanone reduction Vmax=18.8 U/mg, Km=10.8 mmol/L. (R)-2-HPP Vmax=486.8 U/mg, Km=1.6 mmol/L, Ki=16.4 mmol/L, Vopt=299.6 U/mg at 5.1 mmol/L. (S)-2-HPP Vmax=23.1 U/mg, Km=3.6 mmol/L.

selectivity.stereoselectivity: R-selective for alpha-hydroxy ketone reductions; ee >99%, de >99% for most substrates.
selectivity.notes: Compound 43: de >99% syn (R); 44: de >99% anti (R); 45: de >99% anti (S); 46: ee >99% (R); 47: ee >99%, de 87% anti (R); 48: de >99% syn (R).

strategy.notes: Initial rate measurements by spectrophotometry at 340 nm, 60 s, triplicate. Stability: residual activity followed at defined intervals until 50% deactivation.

samples.notes: NADPH consumption or production followed at 340 nm in semi-micro cuvettes (1 mL)

vessel.notes: Semi-micro cuvettes, total volume 1 mL, for spectrophotometric measurements

mixing.notes: Spectrophotometric cuvette assay, no active mixing described
